title: INmune Bio Receives FDA End-of-Phase 2 Meeting Minutes Confirming Integrated Registration Pathway for XPro1595 in Early Alzheimer's Disease
content: INmune Bio, Inc. (Nasdaq: INMB) today announced receipt of official End-of-Phase 2 (EOP2) meeting minutes from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) confirming regulatory alignment on the design of an integrated Phase 2b/3 registration trial for XPro1595 (pegipanermin) in early Alzheimer's disease (AD). The meeting, which took place in January 2026, resolved all major open questions regarding trial design, primary endpoints, patient selection strategy, and sample size. The FDA confirmed no objection to the proposed integrated trial design, in which Phase 2b dose-response data would be used to support a supplemental BLA filing without requiring a standalone Phase 3 trial. The FDA accepted CDR-SB (Clinical Dementia Rating Sum of Boxes) as the sole primary efficacy endpoint, consistent with the regulatory precedent established in recent approvals in the AD space. The Agency explicitly agreed that a composite or co-primary endpoint structure is not required for this program. Regarding patient selection, the FDA accepted INmune Bio's proposed inflammation-based biomarker enrichment strategy. Eligible patients must meet at least two of the following criteria: elevated high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hsCRP ≥ 1.5 mg/L), elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR ≥ 20 mm/hr), elevated HbA1c (≥ 5.7%), or APOE4 carrier status. This enrichment approach is designed to identify patients with the highest likelihood of neuroinflammation-driven disease progression — the population in which XPro1595's mechanism of selectively neutralizing soluble TNF (sTNF) without affecting transmembrane TNF (tmTNF) is expected to provide greatest benefit. The trial is expected to enroll approximately 1,000 participants across sites in the United States and Europe, with a planned 78-week treatment duration. XPro1595's differentiated mechanism addresses a fundamental limitation of conventional anti-TNF biologics: because standard TNF inhibitors neutralize both soluble and membrane-bound TNF indiscriminately, they disrupt TNFR2-mediated neuroprotection and oligodendrocyte survival, making them unsuitable for CNS applications. XPro1595 is a dominant-negative TNF molecule that selectively binds and inactivates sTNF while leaving tmTNF-TNFR signaling intact. Earlier Phase 2 data demonstrated statistically significant reductions in neuroinflammatory biomarkers including YKL-40, MCP-1, and eotaxin-3, alongside improvements in hippocampal volume and white matter integrity on MRI in biomarker-enriched participants. The Company expects to initiate the integrated Phase 2b/3 trial in the second half of 2026, pending IND amendment acceptance. No new clinical holds or safety requirements were placed on the program as a result of the EOP2 meeting.

title: Adalimumab versus Conventional Immunosuppression for Corticosteroid Sparing in Non-Infectious Uveitis: Results of the ADVISE Randomized Controlled Trial
organization: Ophthalmology (Journal of the American Academy of Ophthalmology)
content: Background: Conventional immunosuppressive agents (methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil) are first-line corticosteroid-sparing therapy for non-infectious uveitis, but long-term disease control is achieved in fewer than 60% of patients. Adalimumab (ADA) is FDA-approved for non-infectious intermediate, posterior, and panuveitis based on the VISUAL I and VISUAL II trials, but head-to-head comparative effectiveness data against conventional agents has been lacking. The ADVISE trial was designed to address this evidence gap. Methods: ADVISE was a multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial enrolling 227 adults with active or recently active non-infectious intermediate, posterior, or panuveitis requiring corticosteroid sparing. Patients were randomized 1:1 to adalimumab 40mg subcutaneous every two weeks (n=114) or investigator-selected conventional immunosuppression (methotrexate 15–25mg/week or mycophenolate mofetil 2–3g/day; n=113). The primary endpoint was treatment success at 6 months, defined as corticosteroid tapering to ≤7.5mg/day prednisone equivalent with no uveitis flare and no treatment failure. Results: At 6 months, treatment success was achieved in 78 of 114 adalimumab-treated patients (68.4%) versus 61 of 113 conventional immunosuppression patients (54.0%; p=0.029). At 12 months, corticosteroid discontinuation was achieved in 55.3% of adalimumab patients versus 40.0% of conventional immunosuppression patients (p=0.028). Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was maintained or improved in 87.7% of adalimumab patients versus 78.1% of controls (p=0.047). Treatment-emergent adverse events were similar between arms; injection site reactions occurred in 18.4% of the adalimumab group and were generally mild. Four patients in the conventional immunosuppression arm discontinued due to hepatotoxicity (3 methotrexate, 1 mycophenolate). Conclusions: Adalimumab demonstrated statistically superior corticosteroid sparing compared to conventional immunosuppression in non-infectious uveitis at both 6 and 12 months. These data support earlier deployment of adalimumab in the treatment algorithm for patients with intermediate, posterior, or panuveitis, particularly in those who are at elevated risk from prolonged corticosteroid exposure. The findings are relevant to uveitis subtypes regardless of etiology (idiopathic, sarcoid-associated, or HLA-B27-associated), as subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent treatment effects across disease subtype.

title: Certolizumab Pegol Efficacy Is Independent of Rheumatoid Factor Seropositivity and Prior TNF Inhibitor Exposure: A Post-hoc Analysis of the REALISTIC Phase IIIb Trial
content: Objectives: Rheumatoid factor (RF) seropositivity — particularly at high titers — is associated with more aggressive disease course and historically lower biologic response rates in rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The structural uniqueness of certolizumab pegol (CZP), a PEGylated anti-TNF Fab' fragment lacking an Fc region, led to the hypothesis that CZP may retain efficacy in high-RF patients where Fc-containing antibodies might be partially neutralized by RF binding. This post-hoc analysis of the REALISTIC trial examined CZP clinical response rates across RF strata and prior TNF inhibitor (TNFi) exposure status. Methods: REALISTIC (NCT01257256) was a Phase IIIb, open-label, multicenter study evaluating CZP in a real-world RA population. This post-hoc analysis included 930 patients with available baseline RF data. Patients were stratified by RF level: seronegative (<14 kU/L), low-positive (14–59 kU/L), moderate-positive (60–179 kU/L), and high-positive (≥180 kU/L). Clinical response was assessed using ACR20, ACR50, ACR70, and DAS28-CRP remission (<2.6) at weeks 12 and 52. TNFi-naive and TNFi-experienced subgroups were analyzed separately. Results: ACR20 response rates at week 52 were 71.3%, 68.9%, 70.1%, and 69.4% in seronegative, low-positive, moderate-positive, and high-positive RF groups, respectively (p=0.94 across strata). DAS28-CRP remission rates at week 52 were similarly consistent: 28.4%, 26.7%, 27.8%, and 27.1% across RF strata (p=0.89). In TNFi-experienced patients (n=312), ACR20 response at week 52 was 61.2% compared to 73.4% in TNFi-naive patients (n=618), a statistically significant difference (p=0.003), but within the TNFi-experienced subgroup, response rates were not influenced by RF level. DAS28-CRP low disease activity (≤3.2) at week 12 was achieved in 44.7% of TNFi-experienced high-RF patients, comparable to 46.1% in TNFi-naive high-RF patients. Discussion: The Fc-free structure of CZP likely prevents direct binding by IgM and IgG rheumatoid factors, which could theoretically neutralize or accelerate clearance of Fc-containing anti-TNF antibodies at high RF titers. These data support CZP's use as a preferred anti-TNF agent in high-RF, TNFi-experienced RA patients — a population for whom clinical response to subsequent biologics can be difficult to predict.

title: Adjunctive Fluoxetine in ICU Patients with Severe Sepsis: A Single-Center, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Randomized Trial
content: Background: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) exert immunometabolic effects beyond monoamine reuptake inhibition, including suppression of NF-κB activation in macrophages and consequent reductions in pro-inflammatory cytokine transcription. Observational data have suggested that pre-ICU SSRI use is associated with lower inflammatory cytokine burden and improved sepsis outcomes, but prospective interventional data are lacking. This trial evaluated fluoxetine as an adjunct to standard sepsis care in ICU patients. Methods: Adult patients admitted to the medical ICU with severe sepsis (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment [SOFA] score ≥6) were randomized to fluoxetine 40mg/day via nasogastric tube (n=23) or placebo (n=23) for 14 days in addition to standard protocolized sepsis management. Primary outcomes were vasopressor duration and ICU length of stay. Secondary outcomes included serum TNF-α, IL-1β, and procalcitonin at days 1, 3, and 7, and 28-day all-cause mortality. Results: Vasopressor duration was significantly shorter in the fluoxetine arm (6.2 days, IQR 4.1–8.9) versus placebo (7.9 days, IQR 5.6–10.7; p<0.001). ICU length of stay was reduced in the fluoxetine group (9.4 vs. 12.1 days; p=0.012). Serum TNF-α was significantly lower at day 7 in the fluoxetine arm (48.3 pg/mL vs. 91.7 pg/mL; p=0.004), as was IL-1β (12.1 vs. 22.6 pg/mL; p=0.008) and procalcitonin (4.2 vs. 7.8 ng/mL; p=0.019). Twenty-eight-day mortality was 8.7% in the fluoxetine group versus 17.4% in placebo (absolute difference 8.7%; p=0.381). The trial was not powered to detect a mortality difference. No episodes of QTc prolongation >500ms, serotonin syndrome, or SSRI-attributable bleeding events were observed. Gastrointestinal motility was similar between groups. Discussion: The reduction in vasopressor duration and ICU LOS alongside significant TNF-α suppression supports a biologically plausible anti-inflammatory mechanism for fluoxetine in sepsis. The immunometabolic mechanism — NF-κB inhibition reducing macrophage-derived TNF-α, IL-1β, and procalcitonin — is mechanistically distinct from antibiotic or hemodynamic management strategies and may represent a complementary pathway. A larger multicenter trial powered for mortality is warranted.

title: Montelukast versus Coenzyme Q10 as Adjunctive Anti-inflammatory Therapy in ICU Sepsis: An Open-Label Randomized Controlled Trial
content: Background: Both montelukast (a cysteinyl leukotriene type 1 [CysLT1] receptor antagonist) and coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10, a mitochondrial antioxidant) have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical and small clinical studies, but their comparative efficacy as adjunctive sepsis therapy has not been evaluated. Leukotriene-mediated signaling contributes to systemic inflammatory cascade amplification in sepsis, including downstream TNF-α and interleukin release, making CysLT1 blockade a mechanistically rational intervention. Methods: Ninety adult ICU patients meeting Sepsis-3 criteria were randomized in a 1:1:1 design to montelukast 10mg/day (n=30), CoQ10 200mg twice daily (n=30), or standard care control (n=30). Treatment duration was 10 days. Primary endpoint was 28-day all-cause mortality. Secondary endpoints included serum TNF-α, malondialdehyde (MDA), IL-6, and Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score at days 3, 7, and 10. Results: Twenty-eight-day mortality was 23.3% with montelukast, 40.0% with CoQ10, and 56.7% with standard care. Montelukast was significantly superior to control (p=0.024; RR 0.41, 95% CI 0.19–0.88). CoQ10 showed a non-significant trend toward reduced mortality versus control (p=0.17). Serum TNF-α at day 7 was 41.2 pg/mL in the montelukast group versus 88.6 pg/mL in controls (p=0.001) and 67.3 pg/mL in the CoQ10 group (p=0.04 vs. control). MDA was significantly reduced in both treatment arms versus control at day 7. SOFA scores improved significantly in the montelukast arm by day 7 (mean reduction 3.1 vs. 1.4 in controls; p=0.008). Adverse events: No adverse events were observed in the montelukast group. Hypotension occurred in 6 of 30 CoQ10 patients (20%), two of whom required vasopressor escalation and one of whom was withdrawn from the study. Conclusions: Montelukast demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in 28-day ICU mortality and inflammatory biomarkers including TNF-α compared to standard care. The open-label design and single-center setting are limitations; blinded multicenter replication is needed. The 20% hypotension rate with CoQ10 warrants caution in hemodynamically unstable ICU populations.

title: Maraviroc as Adjunctive Immunomodulatory Therapy in Non-Severe Hospitalized COVID-19: A Proof-of-Concept Phase 2 Randomized Controlled Trial
content: Background: Maraviroc is a CCR5 chemokine receptor antagonist approved for HIV-1 infection that reduces immune cell trafficking to sites of inflammation and modulates downstream pro-inflammatory cytokine cascades, including TNF-α and IL-6. Given that severe COVID-19 pathophysiology involves CCR5-expressing macrophage and T-cell infiltration into pulmonary tissue driving cytokine storm, we evaluated maraviroc's potential to attenuate disease progression in hospitalized non-severe patients before the development of hypoxia requiring intensive intervention. Methods: Thirty-three adults hospitalized with confirmed non-severe COVID-19 (SpO2 ≥94% on room air, no ICU requirement at enrollment) were randomized 2:1 to maraviroc 300mg twice daily plus standard of care (n=22) or standard of care alone (n=11) for 10 days. Primary endpoint was progression to severe COVID-19 (SpO2 <94% requiring supplemental oxygen or ICU admission) within 28 days. Secondary endpoints included serum TNF-α, IL-6, D-dimer, CRP, lymphocyte count, and time to clinical recovery. Results: Disease progression occurred in 2 of 22 maraviroc patients (9.1%) versus 6 of 11 control patients (54.5%), corresponding to a 2.8-fold reduction in progression risk (RR 0.17, 95% CI 0.04–0.68; p=0.013). Serum TNF-α at day 7 was significantly reduced in the maraviroc arm (22.4 pg/mL vs. 61.8 pg/mL; p=0.003). IL-6 was also significantly reduced (18.7 vs. 49.3 pg/mL; p=0.008). Time to clinical recovery was shorter in the maraviroc group (median 7 days vs. 11 days; p=0.031). No Grade 3 or 4 adverse events occurred in the maraviroc arm. In the control arm, three patients required ICU admission for acute hypoxic respiratory failure. Discussion: Maraviroc's existing HIV safety profile, oral bioavailability, and immunomodulatory mechanism make it an attractive repurposing candidate for cytokine storm-associated complications. The small sample size is a primary limitation; however, the effect size is large enough to support a Phase 3 investigation. CCR5 blockade represents a mechanism distinct from antivirals (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) and anti-inflammatory agents (baricitinib, dexamethasone) currently used in COVID-19, suggesting potential additive utility.

title: Safety, Tolerability, Pharmacokinetics, and Preliminary Efficacy of SOR102, an Oral Bispecific Anti-TNF/IL-23 Single-Domain Antibody, in Healthy Volunteers and Adults with Active Ulcerative Colitis: Phase 1 Results
content: Background: SOR102 is a first-in-class oral bispecific single-domain antibody (nanobody) that dually inhibits tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) and interleukin-23 subunit p19 (IL-23p19), two cytokines with validated roles in ulcerative colitis (UC) pathogenesis. Unlike conventional biologic antibodies, single-domain nanobodies are small enough to survive gastrointestinal transit when administered orally in enteric-coated formulations, enabling luminal delivery and topical mucosal effect. This Phase 1 study evaluated SOR102 in healthy volunteers (Part A) and adults with moderately active UC (Part B). Methods: Part A enrolled 24 healthy volunteers in a single ascending dose (SAD) and multiple ascending dose (MAD) design across four dose levels (25mg, 75mg, 200mg, 500mg). Part B enrolled 36 adults with moderately active UC (Mayo score 6–10) randomized 3:1 to active SOR102 (75mg [n=9], 200mg [n=9], or 500mg [n=9]) or placebo (n=9) for 42 days. Primary endpoints were safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics. Exploratory efficacy endpoints were clinical response (≥3-point Mayo score reduction plus ≥30% reduction from baseline) and clinical remission (Mayo score ≤2) at day 42. Results: SOR102 was well tolerated at all dose levels in both parts. No dose-limiting toxicities, serious adverse events, or discontinuations due to adverse events were observed. Systemic absorption was low (mean Cmax <15 ng/mL across dose levels), consistent with a primarily intraluminal mechanism of action. Clinical response at day 42 was 43.0% at 75mg, 52.4% at 200mg, and 56.3% at 500mg, versus 17.0% for placebo. Clinical remission at day 42 was 22.2%, 33.3%, and 33.3% at the three active dose levels versus 0% for placebo. Fecal calprotectin decreased by a mean of 48% from baseline in active treatment groups versus an increase of 12% in placebo. Conclusions: SOR102 demonstrated a favorable safety and pharmacokinetic profile with clinical response rates at day 42 consistent with early-phase data for injectable anti-TNF biologics. The oral route of administration and dual TNF/IL-23 inhibition represent meaningful differentiation in the IBD biologic landscape. A Phase 2 dose-finding trial is planned for initiation in the second half of 2026.

title: Proximal-to-Distal Endoscopic Healing Pattern with Anti-TNF Therapy in Ulcerative Colitis: Pooled Post-hoc Analysis of the HIBISCUS I, HIBISCUS II, and GARDENIA Trials
content: Background: Endoscopic healing is an established treatment target in ulcerative colitis (UC), but the spatial pattern by which anti-TNF therapy achieves colonic mucosal healing has not been systematically characterized. Understanding whether healing proceeds uniformly or in a segmental pattern has implications for how endoscopic endpoints are designed and scored in clinical trials. Methods: We performed a pooled post-hoc analysis of individual patient-level data from HIBISCUS I (golimumab induction, n=103), HIBISCUS II (golimumab maintenance, n=99), and GARDENIA (adalimumab rescue therapy, n=98), yielding 300 UC patients with complete segmental endoscopic assessments at baseline and week 6 or 14. Each patient underwent full colonoscopy with standardized segmental scoring across five colonic regions (cecum/ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid, rectum). Endoscopic assessment used both conventional Mayo Endoscopic Score (MES) and the modified UCEIS. Results: In patients achieving treatment response, healing was significantly more complete in proximal segments than distal segments at the primary timepoint (week 6 in induction trials; week 14 in maintenance). Rectosigmoid healing lagged by a mean of 2.3 weeks compared to proximal colonic healing in responders. Conventional MES (based on the most affected segment) detected treatment effects at week 6 with greater sensitivity than segmental scoring (responder rate: 47.3% vs. 31.8% using respective scoring methods). Combined MES plus patient-reported outcome measure PRO2 (rectal bleeding + stool frequency) outperformed PRO2 alone as a composite endpoint (28.6% composite response rate vs. 13.3% PRO2-only rate; p=0.006). Conclusions: Anti-TNF therapy induces a proximal-to-distal colonic healing gradient in UC. Conventional MES, which captures the most severely affected segment at any colonic location, is a more sensitive clinical trial endpoint than segmental scoring methodologies. Future trial designs for anti-TNF and related mechanisms should account for this healing gradient when selecting endoscopic assessment timepoints and composite endpoint components.

title: Dexmedetomidine Attenuates Endothelial Glycocalyx Degradation and TNF-α Release During Cardiopulmonary Bypass in Valvular Surgery: A Prospective Randomized Controlled Trial
organization: Journal of Cardiothoracic and Vascular Anesthesia
content: Background: Cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB) triggers a systemic inflammatory response characterized by complement activation, endothelial glycocalyx shedding, and pro-inflammatory cytokine release including TNF-α. Glycocalyx degradation, measured by plasma syndecan-1 concentration, is associated with post-CPB endothelial dysfunction, microvascular permeability, and adverse cardiac outcomes. Dexmedetomidine, a selective α2-adrenergic receptor agonist, attenuates sympathoadrenal responses and has been associated with reduced inflammatory cytokine levels in cardiac surgery, but its specific effects on glycocalyx integrity during valvular surgery with CPB have not been evaluated. Methods: Fifty-eight adults undergoing elective valvular surgery (mitral or aortic valve repair or replacement) with CPB were randomized to dexmedetomidine infusion (0.4 mcg/kg/hr, initiated at anesthetic induction and continued until 4 hours post-CPB; n=29) or saline infusion (n=29). Blood samples were collected at baseline (pre-induction), at aortic cross-clamp application, at aortic unclamping, and at 1, 6, and 24 hours post-CPB. Primary outcomes were plasma syndecan-1 and TNF-α concentrations. Secondary outcomes were IL-6, E-selectin, and troponin I. Results: Syndecan-1 at aortic unclamping was significantly lower in the dexmedetomidine group (48.4 ng/mL vs. 87.6 ng/mL; p=0.001), indicating attenuated glycocalyx shedding during the ischemia-reperfusion event. TNF-α was significantly reduced at aortic unclamping (32.1 pg/mL vs. 61.4 pg/mL; p=0.003) and at 1 hour post-CPB (28.7 pg/mL vs. 54.2 pg/mL; p=0.006) in the dexmedetomidine arm. IL-6 was lower at 6 hours post-CPB (88.3 vs. 147.2 pg/mL; p=0.014). Post-operative troponin I at 24 hours was significantly lower in the dexmedetomidine group (1.84 vs. 3.21 ng/mL; p=0.022), suggesting reduced myocardial injury. ICU length of stay was shorter in the dexmedetomidine group (26.4 vs. 34.8 hours; p=0.031). Conclusions: Dexmedetomidine infusion during valvular surgery with CPB significantly attenuated endothelial glycocalyx degradation and TNF-α release at the critical perioperative ischemia-reperfusion event. The glycocalyx-protective effect adds mechanistic insight beyond conventional anti-inflammatory action and may partially explain dexmedetomidine's previously reported cardiac protective effects in cardiac surgery populations.

title: Combined Esketamine and Dexmedetomidine Attenuate Pulmonary Inflammatory Injury and Improve Respiratory Mechanics During One-Lung Ventilation in COPD Patients Undergoing Thoracoscopic Surgery
content: Background: One-lung ventilation (OLV) during video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) is associated with significant pulmonary inflammatory injury, particularly in patients with pre-existing COPD, in whom baseline airway inflammation and impaired mucociliary clearance amplify ischemia-reperfusion injury and cytokine release from the non-ventilated lung. TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8 are the dominant early inflammatory mediators in OLV-related lung injury. Esketamine (S-ketamine), an NMDA receptor antagonist, and dexmedetomidine, an α2-adrenergic receptor agonist, each independently reduce neuroinflammatory and peripheral cytokine signaling, but their combined effect during OLV in COPD has not been evaluated. Methods: Sixty COPD patients (GOLD stage II–III) undergoing elective VATS for pulmonary resection were randomized to esketamine 0.5mg/kg IV bolus plus dexmedetomidine 0.5 mcg/kg/hr infusion (combination, n=20), esketamine alone (n=20), or saline control (n=20). OLV duration ranged 90–150 minutes. Blood samples were collected at pre-OLV baseline, at 60 minutes of OLV, at end of OLV, and at 2 and 24 hours post-operatively. Primary outcomes were serum TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, and malondialdehyde (MDA). Secondary outcomes were dynamic pulmonary compliance, oxygenation index (PaO2/FiO2), peak airway pressure, and extubation time. Results: The combination arm demonstrated significant reductions in all primary inflammatory markers versus control at end of OLV: TNF-α (38.4 vs. 89.7 pg/mL; p<0.001), IL-6 (44.1 vs. 102.3 pg/mL; p<0.001), IL-8 (52.7 vs. 121.4 pg/mL; p<0.001), and MDA (4.3 vs. 8.9 nmol/mL; p<0.001). Esketamine alone produced intermediate reductions significantly greater than control for TNF-α and IL-8 but not IL-6. Dexmedetomidine alone was not included as a separate arm. Oxygenation index at end of OLV was significantly higher in the combination arm (312 vs. 218 mmHg; p<0.001). Dynamic pulmonary compliance was preserved in the combination arm (mean decline of 4.2 cmH2O·L/s vs. 11.7 cmH2O·L/s in controls; p<0.001). Extubation was achieved at a mean of 8.3 hours post-operatively in the combination group versus 13.6 hours in controls (p=0.004). Conclusions: The combination of esketamine and dexmedetomidine synergistically attenuated TNF-α-driven pulmonary inflammatory injury during OLV in COPD patients and significantly improved intraoperative oxygenation and lung mechanics. The additive effect beyond either agent alone suggests mechanistic complementarity between NMDA receptor antagonism and α2-adrenergic modulation of neuroinflammatory cytokine cascades.

title: Alpha-Lipoic Acid Supplementation Reduces Cardiomyocyte Inflammatory and Fibrotic Biomarkers and Improves Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction in Diabetic Ischemic Cardiomyopathy: A Double-Blind Randomized Controlled Trial
content: Background: Ischemic cardiomyopathy (ICM) in patients with type 2 diabetes (T2DM) is characterized by a state of chronic low-grade inflammation superimposed on ischemic myocardial injury, with elevated TNF-α, CRP, TGF-β1, and matrix metalloproteinase-2 (MMP-2) contributing to progressive cardiac fibrosis and ventricular dysfunction. Alpha-lipoic acid (ALA), a naturally occurring dithiol compound functioning as a mitochondrial antioxidant, inhibits NF-κB activation and downstream TNF-α transcription while scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) that drive TGF-β1-mediated fibroblast activation. This trial evaluated ALA supplementation on cardiac inflammatory and fibrotic biomarkers and echocardiographic outcomes in diabetic ICM patients. Methods: Sixty adults with T2DM and ICM (left ventricular ejection fraction [LVEF] 25–40%, documented coronary artery disease on angiography, HbA1c 6.5–10%) were randomized to ALA 600mg/day orally (n=30) or placebo (n=30) for 12 weeks, in addition to optimized guideline-directed medical therapy for both heart failure and diabetes. Primary outcomes were changes in serum TNF-α, CRP, TGF-β1, and MMP-2 from baseline to week 12. Secondary outcomes were change in LVEF by transthoracic echocardiography and 6-minute walk distance. Results: ALA significantly reduced serum TNF-α (from 62.4 to 31.8 pg/mL; p<0.001), CRP (from 8.7 to 4.2 mg/L; p<0.001), TGF-β1 (from 28.3 to 16.9 ng/mL; p=0.002), and MMP-2 (from 412 to 287 ng/mL; p=0.001) versus placebo (no significant changes). LVEF improved significantly in the ALA group (from 31.2% to 36.1%; p=0.018) versus placebo (30.8% to 28.4%; p=0.41), with a mean between-group LVEF difference of 7.7 percentage points at week 12 (p<0.05). Six-minute walk distance improved by a mean of 48m in the ALA group versus 6m in placebo (p=0.009). ALA was well tolerated; no serious adverse events or hypoglycemia attributable to ALA were observed. Discussion: The simultaneous reductions in TNF-α, CRP, TGF-β1, and MMP-2 alongside LVEF improvement suggest that ALA's cardiac benefit in diabetic ICM may operate through both anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic pathways. The TGF-β1 and MMP-2 reductions are particularly noteworthy, implicating extracellular matrix remodeling as a potentially modifiable target in diabetic cardiomyopathy.

title: Anti-Inflammatory and Symptomatic Effects of Low-Dose Colchicine in Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction: An Observational Pilot Study
organization: ESC Heart Failure (European Society of Cardiology)
content: Background: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) is characterized by chronic systemic and myocardial inflammation, with elevated IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α contributing to endothelial dysfunction, cardiomyocyte stiffness, and diastolic impairment. Colchicine, a microtubule polymerization inhibitor that suppresses NLRP3 inflammasome activation and downstream IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α signaling, has demonstrated cardiovascular anti-inflammatory benefit in pericarditis and post-myocardial infarction settings but has not been evaluated in HFpEF. Methods: One hundred twenty-six adults with HFpEF (LVEF ≥50%, NYHA class II–III, NT-proBNP >125 pg/mL) were enrolled in an observational prospective cohort and received colchicine 0.5mg/day added to standard HFpEF management for 14 days. Assessments were performed at baseline and at 2 and 4 weeks. Primary outcomes were changes in serum IL-8, IL-6, and TNF-α. Secondary outcomes were patient-reported anxiety (HAMA), depression (HAMD), and health-related quality of life (Kansas City Cardiomyopathy Questionnaire, KCCQ) at 1 month. Results: Serum IL-8 was reduced by a mean of 65.5% from baseline at 2 weeks (from 48.3 pg/mL to 16.7 pg/mL; p=0.001). IL-6 was reduced by 42.3% at 2 weeks (p=0.003). TNF-α showed a non-significant trend toward reduction (18.7%; p=0.11) at 2 weeks but was significantly reduced at 4 weeks (29.4%; p=0.024). HAMA scores improved significantly at 1 month (mean reduction 4.2 points; p=0.014). HAMD scores similarly improved (mean reduction 3.8 points; p=0.021). KCCQ overall summary score improved by a mean of 8.7 points at 1 month (p=0.003). Gastrointestinal adverse effects (primarily diarrhea) occurred in 9.5% of patients and were managed with dose modification or antidiarrheal agents; no patients required study withdrawal. Limitations: The observational, single-arm design precludes causal inference; a placebo-controlled trial is required. Conclusions: Low-dose colchicine produced rapid and significant reductions in IL-8 and IL-6 in HFpEF patients, alongside meaningful improvements in patient-reported anxiety, depression, and quality of life metrics. The symptomatic improvements in non-cardiac endpoints (HAMA, HAMD, KCCQ) suggest systemic anti-inflammatory benefit extending beyond cardiac biomarkers and support a randomized trial of colchicine in HFpEF.

title: Immunomodulatory Profile of Cannabidiol (Epidiolex) at Steady-State Plasma Concentrations in Solid Organ Transplant Recipients: Ex Vivo T-Cell Cytokine Analysis from a Phase 1 Study
content: Background: Cannabidiol (CBD, Epidiolex; Jazz Pharmaceuticals) is an FDA-approved plant-derived cannabinoid with established anti-seizure efficacy and emerging immunomodulatory properties. Transplant recipients increasingly use CBD for pain management as an alternative to opioids, but CBD's effects on immune function in the context of concurrent calcineurin inhibitor immunosuppression are not well characterized. TNF-α, IL-2, and lymphotoxin alpha (LTA) are key T-effector memory cell cytokines driving allograft rejection; their modulation by CBD could have clinically significant implications for transplant immunosuppression management. Methods: Twenty-three adult solid organ transplant recipients (renal n=14, hepatic n=6, cardiac n=3) who were enrolled in a Phase 1 pharmacokinetic study of Epidiolex received CBD 10mg/kg/day for 14 days. Tacrolimus dosing was not adjusted. Ex vivo cytokine production was assessed from peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) isolated at baseline and at steady state (day 14) using stimulated whole-blood assay (anti-CD3/anti-CD28). Primary outcomes were changes in TNF-α, IL-2, LTA, IL-10, and IL-17 production from T effector memory cells at steady state versus baseline. Secondary outcome was trough tacrolimus level variability. Results: At steady-state CBD concentrations (mean plasma Cmax 203 ng/mL), TNF-α production from T effector memory cells was reduced by a mean of 31.4% versus baseline (p=0.004). IL-2 was reduced by 27.8% (p=0.008). LTA production was reduced by 22.1% (p=0.019). IL-10 production increased by 44.7% (p=0.001). IL-17 was not significantly changed. Tacrolimus trough levels increased by a mean of 38% from baseline (from 6.2 to 8.6 ng/mL; p=0.001), consistent with CBD's known CYP3A4/CYP2C19 inhibitory effects increasing tacrolimus bioavailability. One patient experienced tacrolimus toxicity (AKI, creatinine increase >2× baseline) and required tacrolimus dose reduction; all other patients remained within therapeutic range after dose monitoring. Conclusions: CBD at therapeutic steady-state concentrations significantly suppressed TNF-α, IL-2, and LTA while increasing IL-10 in transplant recipient T effector memory cells — a profile that is additive with calcineurin inhibitor immunosuppression. Clinically, this creates a dual risk: excessive immunosuppression increasing infection risk and tacrolimus toxicity from pharmacokinetic drug-drug interaction. Transplant physicians and pharmacists should be alerted to screen for CBD use, monitor tacrolimus levels closely, and consider empiric dose reduction upon CBD initiation.

title: Protocol for the LIRA-MOOD Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial: Effects of Liraglutide on Depressive Symptoms and Inflammatory Biomarkers in Overweight and Obese Adults with Type 2 Diabetes
content: Background and rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists including liraglutide reduce adipose tissue-mediated chronic inflammation by improving insulin sensitivity and reducing adipokine dysregulation, including reductions in TNF-α, IL-6, and leptin. Epidemiological data suggest a bidirectional relationship between type 2 diabetes (T2DM), obesity, and major depressive disorder (MDD), with pro-inflammatory cytokines — particularly TNF-α — proposed as a shared mechanistic link through the inflammatory model of depression. Liraglutide has demonstrated antidepressant-like effects in preclinical models, and small observational studies have reported mood improvement alongside weight loss in patients initiating GLP-1 therapy, but no adequately powered RCT has examined liraglutide's effect on depressive symptoms as a primary outcome in a T2DM population. Trial design: LIRA-MOOD is a single-center, double-blind, placebo-controlled pilot RCT. Eligible participants are adults aged 18–70 with a diagnosis of T2DM (HbA1c 7.0–10.0%), body mass index ≥27 kg/m², and current major depressive episode (PHQ-9 ≥10) who are not currently receiving antidepressant therapy. Participants will be randomized 1:1 to liraglutide (dose-escalated to 1.8mg/day subcutaneous; n=30) or matching placebo (n=30) for 24 weeks. Primary outcome: Change from baseline in PHQ-9 depression score at week 24. Secondary outcomes: HAMD-17 score, HbA1c, body weight, fasting glucose, and serum inflammatory biomarkers including TNF-α, IL-6, CRP, and adiponectin at weeks 12 and 24. TNF-α is included as a secondary biomarker outcome based on the hypothesis that liraglutide's antidepressant effect is mediated at least partially through suppression of TNF-α-driven neuroinflammation. No efficacy results are available at the time of this protocol publication. Statistical considerations: The pilot trial is powered to detect a minimum clinically important difference of 5 PHQ-9 points (80% power, α=0.05) and to provide sample size estimates for a definitive Phase 3 trial. Enrollment is planned to begin in Q2 2026.
